content: OAuth account linking same-email collision pitfalls: (1) Account takeover via unverified email — an attacker registers johndoe@example.com at a permissive OAuth provider, then signs into a service that auto-links on email match, gaining access to the victim's account; prevention requires checking provider's email_verified flag before auto-linking. (2) Duplicate accounts from case mismatch — providers return mixed-case email (JohnDoe@example.com) while DB has lowercase; case-sensitive match creates a second account; normalize emails to lowercase before matching (RFC 5321 local part is technically case-sensitive but providers treat it as case-insensitive in practice). (3) Email-as-identity assumption breaks with provider changes — user's GitHub email changes and is now different from their Better Auth email; account linking by email alone fails; should link by provider sub/id, not email. (4) Existing password session not invalidated after OAuth link — user links Google but their old password session persists; if password was weak or compromised, OAuth link does not improve security.
